steps.title: More Than Date Night
steps.body:
Connection isn't about grand gestures. It's about the space between you.

Some couples talk for hours but never feel truly seen. Others say ten words a day and feel profoundly connected. Why?

Because connection has two layers:

1. Emotional intimacy — feeling known, understood, and safe
2. Physical intimacy — touch, affection, sex, presence

Most couples focus on the physical when the emotional needs attention. Or vice versa. Today, we look at both.

The question isn't 'Do you still love each other?'

It's 'Do you still LIKE each other?'

Love endures. Liking requires attention. It's the difference between staying together and wanting to be together.
highlights: Do you still LIKE each other?

steps.title: Emotional Intimacy Check
steps.prompt:
Answer honestly:

• When something good happens, is your partner the first person you want to tell?
• When something hard happens, do you feel safe being vulnerable with them?
• When was the last time you had a conversation that wasn't about logistics (kids, money, schedules)?

What do your answers tell you?

steps.title: The Friendship Factor
steps.body:
Gottman's research revealed something counterintuitive: the happiest couples treat each other like best friends.

They know each other's inner worlds — stresses, dreams, pet peeves, favorite movies. They have inside jokes. They're curious about each other's days.

Romance without friendship is a house without a foundation. Friendship without romance is roommates. You need both.

steps.question: Physical intimacy problems are usually really about physical issues.
feedback: Correct. Physical intimacy problems are almost always emotional intimacy problems wearing a physical mask. When couples feel emotionally disconnected, physical connection suffers — not the other way around.
Not quite. Most physical intimacy issues stem from emotional disconnection — feeling unseen, unappreciated, or unsafe. Fix the emotional connection and the physical often follows.

steps.title: The Friendship Audit
steps.prompt:
How well do you know your partner's inner world right now?

• What's their biggest stressor this week?
• What are they excited about?
• What's a small thing that would make their day better?

If you don't know the answers — that's not a failure. It's information. Use it.

steps.title: Physical Intimacy: The Candid Part
steps.body:
Physical intimacy matters. It's not shallow to care about it. It's human.

The question isn't 'How often?' — that's the wrong metric. The question is: does your physical connection feel mutual, wanted, and connecting for both of you?

When it does, it's glue. When it doesn't, it's a wedge.

This isn't about performance. It's about presence.

steps.title: Two Kinds of Rejection
steps.body:
Scenario A: Your partner initiates physically. You're not in the mood. You say, "Not right now" and roll over.

Scenario B: Same situation. You say, "I'm not in that headspace right now, but I love that you want me. Can we just hold each other for a few minutes?"

Same outcome. Completely different message.

Scenario A says: I'm rejecting YOU.
Scenario B says: I'm not in that space, but I'm not rejecting US.

Connection lives in the difference.

steps.title: Connect Today
steps.prompt:
One small action today to strengthen connection:

• Ask about their day and actually listen (no phone, no TV)
• Give a 20-second hug (it releases oxytocin)
• Tell them something you appreciate — specific, not generic

Write your commitment:

steps.title: Tomorrow: The Household Reality
steps.body: Connection is the heart. But tomorrow, we tackle the least romantic — and often most divisive — pillar: the Household. Money, chores, logistics. The stuff that kills romance when it's not working.
